1.synthesis:
Hezekiah son of Ahaz begins to reign in the third year of Hoshea son of Elah of Israel. He is twenty-five years old and reigns twenty-nine years. The evaluation is the highest given to any king in Judah: he does right in the sight of the Lord according to all that David his father had done. He removes the high places — something no other king of Judah before him had done — and breaks down the sacred pillars and cuts down the Asherah pole. He crushes the bronze serpent that Moses had made (Num 21:9), calling it Nehushtan (a piece of bronze), because the Israelites had been burning incense to it up to that time. The narrator's verdict is extraordinary: "He trusted in the Lord, the God of Israel, so that there was none like him among all the kings of Judah after him, nor among those before him." He holds fast to the Lord, does not depart from following him, and keeps the commandments that the Lord commanded Moses. The Lord is with him; wherever he goes out, he prospers. He rebels against the king of Assyria and does not serve him. He strikes down the Philistines as far as Gaza. The fall of Samaria is noted parenthetically as the backdrop to Hezekiah's faithfulness.

John Gill notes the specific mention of the bronze serpent from the Mosaic era: this artifact, preserved for eight centuries, had become an object of idolatrous reverence. Hezekiah's destruction of it — calling it "a mere piece of bronze" — is both iconoclastic and theologically precise. The sacred thing that was once a legitimate instrument of God's grace had been transformed by time and popular piety into an idol; Hezekiah recognizes the transformation and acts. Matthew Henry observes that Hezekiah's reform includes high place removal — what even good kings like Jehoshaphat and Joash had failed to accomplish — making him the standard against which all subsequent kings are measured. Keil and Delitzsch note that "there was none like him among all the kings of Judah after him, nor before him" is an unparalleled evaluation, matched only by the similar absolute positive assessment of Josiah in chapter 23 — both kings receiving the highest possible praise.
1.historical_context: Hezekiah's reign (ca. 726–697 BC) falls in the critical period between the fall of Samaria (722 BC) and Sennacherib's invasion of Judah (701 BC). His religious reforms documented in Chronicles include reopening the temple (closed by Ahaz), reinstituting Levitical worship, celebrating a major Passover (including inviting survivors from the northern kingdom), and removing the high places and altars throughout Judah and Benjamin. The bronze serpent (nehushtan) from Num 21:8–9 had evidently been preserved in the Jerusalem temple or its precinct for centuries; its incense-burning cult is an example of legitimate cultic objects becoming objects of veneration. Hezekiah's "rebellion" against Assyria — withholding tribute — sets up the confrontation with Sennacherib in 701 BC narrated in the following sections. The Philistine campaigns may be connected to asserting control over the coastal plain during a period of Assyrian weakness under Sargon II (705 BC at Sargon's death created a window for rebellion).
1.literary_structure: The introduction to Hezekiah's reign functions as the positive counter to the Ahaz introduction: where Ahaz received the most negative evaluation of any southern king, Hezekiah receives the most positive. The bronze serpent episode (18:4) is structurally important — it connects Hezekiah's reform to the Mosaic era, establishing the theological depth of his clearing of accumulated religious accretions. The narrator's summary statement ("he trusted in the Lord… there was none like him") is placed before the historical narrative of Sennacherib's invasion, ensuring the reader evaluates the crisis through the lens of Hezekiah's character.
1.theological_application: Calvin reads Hezekiah's destruction of the bronze serpent as the paradigmatic act of iconoclastic faithfulness: even objects with legitimate sacred history can become idolatrous when they replace rather than mediate genuine worship of God. The history of Christian worship includes many similar examples where relics, images, or sacred objects that began as aids to devotion have become foci of idolatrous veneration; Hezekiah's calling Nehushtan "a mere piece of bronze" is the appropriate response. Matthew Henry draws the broader principle: reformers must have the courage to remove not only clearly wicked things but also things that have become corrupted over time, even when those things have venerable pedigree. The highest evaluation Hezekiah receives — "none like him before or after" — is precisely because he trusted God completely and acted on that trust with comprehensive reform.
1.christology: The bronze serpent that Hezekiah destroys is the very object that Jesus references in his conversation with Nicodemus: "Just as Moses lifted up the snake in the wilderness, so the Son of Man must be lifted up, that everyone who believes may have eternal life in him" (John 3:14–15). Christ is the antitype of the bronze serpent — the one lifted up on the cross as the means of healing for those who look in faith. But where the bronze serpent itself became an idol that needed destroying, Christ cannot become an idol; he is himself the truth that every idol substitutes for. Hezekiah's iconoclastic act against Nehushtan clears the path for the genuine antitypical fulfillment: not a bronze object that receives incense but the Son of Man lifted up who draws all people to himself (John 12:32).

13.synthesis:
In the fourteenth year of Hezekiah, Sennacherib king of Assyria comes up against all the fortified cities of Judah and takes them. Hezekiah sends to Sennacherib at Lachish acknowledging sin and offering to pay whatever tribute Sennacherib demands. Sennacherib requires three hundred talents of silver and thirty talents of gold, which Hezekiah provides by stripping the temple and his own palace — even the gold overlaying the temple doors. Then Sennacherib sends a large army under his Rabshakeh (field commander) from Lachish to Jerusalem with a massive psychological assault. The Rabshakeh's speech is one of the most extraordinary documents in the ancient world: delivered in Hebrew before the people of Jerusalem, before Hezekiah's negotiating team, it systematically attacks every possible basis of confidence. Do not trust in Egypt — it is a broken reed. Do not trust in the Lord your God — Hezekiah removed his high places and altars. Do not trust in Hezekiah's assurances of divine protection. The Rabshakeh even claims the Lord commanded the Assyrian invasion: "The Lord himself told me to march against this country and destroy it." He offers economic terms — stay and eat your own vine and fig tree — before Assyria relocates you to an equally pleasant land. No god of any nation has delivered from Assyrian power; how will the Lord deliver Jerusalem?

Hezekiah's envoys ask the Rabshakeh to speak in Aramaic rather than Hebrew, since the people on the wall are listening. The Rabshakeh refuses — it is precisely the people he is addressing. His speech is psychological warfare of sophisticated design, targeting every possible source of Judahite confidence. Hezekiah's team returns with torn clothes; Hezekiah himself tears his clothes, covers himself with sackcloth, and goes to the house of the Lord. He sends to Isaiah the prophet.

John Gill provides extensive commentary on the Rabshakeh's theological sophistication: his argument that Hezekiah's removal of the high places removed the places where the Lord was worshiped (misunderstanding centralization reform as destruction of Yahwistic worship) shows that Assyrian intelligence was detailed but theologically misread. Matthew Henry identifies the Rabshakeh's speech as the paradigm of satanic temptation to despair: every argument for trusting God is systematically dismantled, and the conclusion offered is surrender. Keil and Delitzsch document the Assyrian siege of Lachish through the famous palace reliefs — archaeological corroboration of the Kings account.
13.historical_context: Sennacherib's invasion of Judah in 701 BC is one of the best-documented events in the ancient Near East, corroborated by his own detailed annals (the "Taylor Prism"), the Lachish reliefs, and multiple prophetic texts in Isaiah. His annals claim to have trapped Hezekiah "like a bird in a cage" in Jerusalem and to have taken forty-six cities of Judah, deporting over 200,000 people. The tributary payment described in 18:14–16 corresponds to Sennacherib's account of receiving thirty talents of gold and eight hundred talents of silver (the discrepancy in silver may reflect different accounting systems). The Rabshakeh's speech is delivered before the conduit of the Upper Pool, the same location where Isaiah had addressed Ahaz about the Syro-Ephraimite crisis (Isa 7:3) — a deliberate geographic resonance in the narrative. The Lachish siege is independently documented by the famous Assyrian palace reliefs now in the British Museum.
13.literary_structure: The section builds from initial defeat and tribute (the capitulation of 13–16) to the Assyrian escalation (the siege of Jerusalem and the Rabshakeh's speech, 17–37). The shift from Hezekiah's submission to the psychological siege is the narrative surprise: paying the tribute does not buy security. The Rabshakeh's three arguments — do not trust Egypt, do not trust your Lord, do not trust Hezekiah — are presented in full, giving the reader the full rhetorical force of the Assyrian challenge before the divine response in chapter 19. Hezekiah's team's final response — silence, as commanded by the king — sets up the prayer of chapter 19.
13.theological_application: The Rabshakeh's speech is analyzed by Reformed commentators as the most sophisticated spiritual temptation in the historical books. Calvin identifies its key move: it attacks not the people's military strength but their theological hope, systematically demonstrating that every external confidence is misplaced. The appropriate response — silence commanded by Hezekiah, then sackcloth and the house of the Lord — is not despair but the redirection of hope from every false foundation to God alone. Matthew Henry observes that the Rabshakeh's partial theological accuracy (no god has delivered from Assyrian power — true, historically) is used to draw a false conclusion (therefore your Lord cannot either). The critical error is treating Israel's God as a national deity of the same type as the gods that failed; the response requires a theological counter-argument, which Hezekiah will receive from Isaiah.
13.christology: The Rabshakeh's systematic demolition of every human basis for confidence in deliverance anticipates Satan's temptations of Jesus in the wilderness: "If you are the Son of God..." — attacking every category of divine identity and mission through strategic doubt. Where the Rabshakeh's argument is ultimately answered by divine intervention (the angel who strikes down the Assyrian army), Christ's temptations are answered by the word of God alone, without supernatural rescue. But both demonstrate that the final ground of confidence is not human resource, political alliance, or even religious heritage but the character and word of God himself. The Rabshakeh asks "Where is the god of this and that nation?" (18:34); the resurrection of Jesus answers the same form of question definitively.
